title: Trash Of The Titans

jokes.text: D'oh! [Screams]
jokes.setup: Opening title sequence - Homer presumably crashes or gets hurt
jokes.punchline: His signature frustrated exclamation
jokes.explanation: Homer's 'D'oh!' is iconic and perfectly character-integrated but routine at this point in the series

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Christmas-Hanu-Kwanzaa spend phase
jokes.setup: Corporate meeting discussing holiday sales
jokes.punchline: Cynical corporate mashup of all winter holidays into one spending period
jokes.explanation: Sharp satire of corporate holiday commercialization, economical and well-crafted

dialogue.speaker: Corporate Executive
dialogue.line: Gentlemen, we need to maximize our holiday revenue. I propose we merge Christmas, Hanukkah, and Kwanzaa into one mega-spending event called 'Christmakwanzukkah.'

dialogue.speaker: Corporate Executive
dialogue.line: Think of the synergy! One shopping season, triple the guilt, quadruple the debt.

jokes.text: Mom-Dad-Grad gift corridor
jokes.setup: Continuing corporate sales meeting
jokes.punchline: Reducing Mother's Day, Father's Day, and graduation to a sales corridor
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up to first joke, maintaining satirical corporate speak

dialogue.speaker: Board Executive
dialogue.line: We're reducing Mother's Day, Father's Day, and graduation to a sales corridor.

jokes.text: Christmas II
jokes.setup: Need for new religious holidays
jokes.punchline: Treating Christmas like a movie sequel
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist take on sequel culture applied to religion, very quotable

dialogue.speaker: Corporate Executive
dialogue.line: We need new religious holidays. We're treating Christmas like a movie sequel — it comes out every year and nobody wants it anymore.

jokes.text: Spendover. Like Passover, but less talk, more presents
jokes.setup: Brainstorming new holidays
jokes.punchline: Reducing sacred religious observance to consumerism
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay with subversive edge, captures corporate cynicism perfectly

dialogue.speaker: Corporate Executive
dialogue.line: What if we created a new holiday that combines all the religious observances into one big shopping event?

dialogue.speaker: Corporate Executive
dialogue.line: We could call it 'Consumermas' — it's got the spiritual meaning of Christmas, Hanukkah, and Kwanzaa, but mostly just the part where people buy things.

jokes.text: Lisa, don't you ruin another Love Day
jokes.setup: Lisa points out Love Day is just corporate manipulation
jokes.punchline: Marge treats this as Lisa being a chronic holiday spoiler
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Marge's willful ignorance of corporate manipulation

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Love Day is just corporate manipulation.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Lisa, you say that about every holiday.

jokes.text: They didn't have Lord Huggington? It's the same basic bear, Homie
jokes.setup: Homer disappointed in his Love Day gift
jokes.punchline: Homer's specific attachment to brand names for identical products
jokes.explanation: Solid character comedy showing Homer's brand obsession and childish disappointment

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, this is the worst Love Day gift ever! You got me socks? I wanted Duff brand socks!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, they're the exact same socks. They're made by the same company.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No they're not! These say 'Foot Comfort' on the tag. I need Duff brand or they don't keep my feet warm!

jokes.text: Nuh-uh. It isn't filled until it's spilled
jokes.setup: Kids say trash is full and Homer needs to take it out
jokes.punchline: Homer's ridiculous logic to avoid chores with rhyming excuse
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - lazy but with internal consistency and rhyme that makes it memorable

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Can't take out the trash today, it's Tuesday, you see. The trash man only comes on... uh... Wednesday to Thursday. It's a scientific fact.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that makes no sense.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, Marge, Marge. If I take out the trash on a day that rhymes with 'shmaturday', I'll upset the garbage gods. I read it on the internet.

jokes.text: I wuv you... Love you...
jokes.setup: Homer stuffing more trash into overflowing bin
jokes.punchline: Love Day bear getting crushed and voice distorting
jokes.explanation: Simple visual gag that works but isn't particularly memorable or creative

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Huh, it still fits.

dialogue.speaker: Sir Loves-A-Lot
dialogue.line: Help me... I'm being crushed... by the weight... of your garbage...

jokes.text: Stupid trash. Rotten. Stinky. Hate world. Revenge soon. Take out on everyone.
jokes.setup: Homer struggling with overflowing trash
jokes.punchline: Homer's caveman-like muttering escalating to global revenge fantasy
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Homer's primitive thought process escalating absurdly

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why won't this trash go down? Stupid garbage... stupid lid...

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Grrr... I'll show this trash. I'll show everyone. First the trash company, then the mayor, then... the whole world will pay!

jokes.text: You guys are the lousiest garbagemen ever! You trash-eatin' stink bags!
jokes.setup: Homer missing the garbage truck
jokes.punchline: His over-the-top insults that will backfire spectacularly
jokes.explanation: Sets up the main conflict perfectly - Homer's impulsive anger creating his own problems

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey! Hey! Wait! Wait! Come back here, you stupid garbage truck!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You're dumber than a sack of hammers! You're uglier than a butt! Your mother was a dump!

dialogue.speaker: Garbage Truck Driver
dialogue.line: That's it, Simpson. You're off the route.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, no! I didn't mean it! Please come back!

jokes.text: I don't know. A lot of people were yelling stuff
jokes.setup: Garbagemen confronting Homer about insults
jokes.punchline: Homer immediately backpedaling and playing dumb
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer cowardice - brave when alone, cowardly when confronted

dialogue.speaker: Garbageman
dialogue.line: Hey! We heard what you said about us!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What? I didn't say anything.

dialogue.speaker: Garbageman
dialogue.line: You called us 'garbage men'!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No, no, no... I said you were 'great men'! Great men!

jokes.text: Didn't you learn anything from Love Day? That was yesterday, moron
jokes.setup: Garbageman invoking Love Day's message
jokes.punchline: Homer dismissing love and peace after 24 hours
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback that exposes how quickly people abandon holiday messages, very sharp writing

dialogue.speaker: Garbageman
dialogue.line: Remember, on Love Day, we must all embrace love and peace.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, yeah, love and peace. Sure, whatever.

dialogue.speaker: Garbageman
dialogue.line: It's a day to celebrate harmony among all people.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That was yesterday. Today I'm back to hating everyone.

jokes.text: Hey, I'd rather live in a dump than in a world run by snooty garbagemen
jokes.setup: Marge asking about the trash situation
jokes.punchline: Homer's false bravado that will literally come true
jokes.explanation: Great dramatic irony - audience knows this will backfire, sets up episode perfectly

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, what about all this trash?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Don't worry, Marge. I'll handle it. I'm not afraid of a little garbage. In fact, I'm gonna go out there right now and show that trash who's boss!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Nothing's gonna stop me. I'm unstoppable!

jokes.text: I never apologize, Lisa. I'm sorry, but that's just the way I am
jokes.setup: Lisa suggesting Homer could apologize
jokes.punchline: Homer apologizing while claiming he never apologizes
jokes.explanation: Perfect self-contradiction joke with excellent structure - Homer's lack of self-awareness crystallized

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: You could apologize.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Apologize? I never apologize, Lisa. It's a sign of weakness.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But I'm sorry, and it won't happen again.

jokes.text: So long, chicken bone. See you in hell, brussels sprouts. No room for you, Jell-O. Good-bye, fine china
jokes.setup: Family clearing table by throwing things away
jokes.punchline: Treating discarded items like dramatic farewells, escalating to expensive china
jokes.explanation: Good escalating absurdity with nice rhythm, shows how wasteful they've become

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Goodbye, sweet potatoes. You were delicious.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Farewell, gravy boat. We hardly knew ye.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh, stop being so dramatic about the garbage.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Adieu, fancy china! Our love was too pure for this world!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, that was a wedding gift from your mother.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I know! That's what makes it so tragic!

jokes.text: Then you might want to close your windows before the sun hits Diaper Hill
jokes.setup: Ned complaining about odors
jokes.punchline: Homer casually referencing a hill made of dirty diapers in their yard
jokes.explanation: Excellent visual comedy reference delivered with perfect casual tone, memorable phrase

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Well, I declare! What is that horrible stench?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh, that's just the diaper hill out back. We've been saving them for composting.

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: A hill of... dirty diapers?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yep. Marge says we'll get to it eventually.

jokes.text: Look, Daddy. I'm the king of the mountain!
jokes.setup: Discussion of Diaper Hill
jokes.punchline: Innocent child playing on pile of dirty diapers
jokes.explanation: Solid gross-out humor with innocent child contrast, works but not exceptional

dialogue.speaker: Rod Flanders
dialogue.line: Gee, mister! What's Diaper Hill?

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: It's a big pile of dirty diapers, son.

dialogue.speaker: Rod Flanders
dialogue.line: Gosh! Can I play there?

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Sure, knock yourself out, kid.

jokes.text: That's not the way she tells it
jokes.setup: Bart says crazy lady in trash pile attacked him
jokes.punchline: Homer treating it like a he-said-she-said dispute
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer obliviousness - treating insane situation as normal domestic dispute

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, the crazy lady in the trash pile attacked me!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Now Bart, I wasn't there. So I'm gonna take the trash lady's side.

jokes.text: It's like the measles. Good to get it out of the way
jokes.setup: Bart has the plague
jokes.punchline: Homer treating medieval disease like childhood illness
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about serious disease treated casually, good character blindness

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: There, there, boy. You'll be fine. Why, I had the plague when I was your age.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that's impossible. The plague hasn't been around for centuries.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, it felt like the plague. My whole body ached, I had a fever, and these weird bumps all over me.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: That was chickenpox.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Plague, chickenpox... what's the difference? The point is, I survived it, and so will Bart.

jokes.text: Yeah, but I'm always secretly disappointed when you do
jokes.setup: Kids pointing out Homer tells them to apologize
jokes.punchline: Homer admitting he prefers when they don't follow his advice
jokes.explanation: Brilliant parenting insight - Homer's contradictory nature and bad influence captured perfectly

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh, apologize? That's what I told you to do. But between you and me, I prefer when you don't listen to me.

jokes.text: It's just like David and Goliath, only this time David won
jokes.setup: Homer celebrating victory over city hall
jokes.punchline: Homer not knowing David DID win in the biblical story
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer ignorance of basic cultural knowledge, perfectly executed

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Take that, city hall! I'm a David, and I just beat your Goliath!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, David did win against Goliath in the Bible.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh. Well, I'm still a winner!

jokes.text: [Thinking] Oh. I know. I heard it too
jokes.setup: Homer's David and Goliath mistake
jokes.punchline: Lisa's internal reaction to her father's ignorance
jokes.explanation: Nice character moment showing Lisa's embarrassment and intelligence

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, David and Goliath is a biblical story, not a historical event.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Whatever, Lisa. Goliath was huge!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: (sighs internally) How do I explain this to someone who thinks the Bible is a history book?

jokes.text: That rhymes, Marge, and you know it rhymes. Admit it!
jokes.setup: Homer saying he stands tall instead of crawls
jokes.punchline: Homer demanding recognition for his accidental rhyme
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer pride in tiny accomplishments, childlike need for praise

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I stand tall instead of crawl.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey, I just made a rhyme! Where's my prize?

jokes.text: But this isn't like a loan application or a will!
jokes.setup: Marge pointing out Homer has signed her name before
jokes.punchline: Homer revealing he's forged important documents
jokes.explanation: Good escalation revealing Homer's casual document fraud as normal behavior

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you forged my signature on the electric bill!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, yeah, but that's nothing. I've also forged your signature on the mortgage, the car loan, and our marriage certificate.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: You forged our MARRIAGE certificate?!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, relax. I'm pretty sure we're still married. The important thing is I saved myself a trip to the DMV.

jokes.text: Oh, oh, oh, I get it. Put on a big show for the cameras. What cameras?
jokes.setup: Homer being confrontational at city hall
jokes.punchline: Homer imagining persecution that doesn't exist
jokes.explanation: Solid character moment showing Homer's delusional self-importance

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why are you all staring at me? What did I do? Is this about the parking meter? Because that was an accident!

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: Mr. Simpson, nobody's accusing you of anything. We just need you to sign this form.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A form? Oh sure, a form! Next you'll want my fingerprints, then my DNA, then you'll clone me and make me work in your underground nuclear salt mines!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that's not—

jokes.text: Hey, stop that! You'll never silence me!
jokes.setup: Homer making dramatic speech
jokes.punchline: Bird apparently interrupting him, Homer fighting it
jokes.explanation: Basic physical comedy that works but isn't particularly memorable

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I have something very important to say!

dialogue.speaker: Bird
dialogue.line: *squawk*

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey! Get outta here!

jokes.text: Okay. But this is where you register as a sex offender
jokes.setup: Homer wanting to register to run for office
jokes.punchline: He's accidentally at wrong, much more serious registry
jokes.explanation: Dark humor that works through misdirection, though the setup is a bit forced

dialogue.speaker: City Hall Clerk
dialogue.line: Name?

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Homer Jay Simpson.

dialogue.speaker: City Hall Clerk
dialogue.line: Address?

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: 742 Evergreen Terrace.

dialogue.speaker: City Hall Clerk
dialogue.line: You're now registered.

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Registered for what?

dialogue.speaker: City Hall Clerk
dialogue.line: As a sex offender.

jokes.text: If you don't, he'll beat us. Why, you little- No one's gonna beat you, Son. [Softly] You're gonna get such a beating
jokes.setup: Bart campaigning for Homer with threat
jokes.punchline: Homer denying it publicly then confirming it privately
jokes.explanation: Good contradiction between public and private behavior, classic Simpsons dark humor

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, if you don't let me stay up late, I'm telling everyone at school you pick your nose.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I do NOT pick my nose!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, not in public.

jokes.text: What am I, the answer man? Just vote for me
jokes.setup: Voter asking what Homer will do different
jokes.punchline: Homer refusing to explain his platform
jokes.explanation: Good political satire - candidate avoiding substance, pure Homer laziness

dialogue.speaker: Voter
dialogue.line: What will you do differently?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, I'm not going to waste your time with a lot of fancy promises. I'm just going to tell you what I'm going to do: absolutely nothing.

jokes.text: You know, I don't know Ray Patterson, but he's no Ray Patterson
jokes.setup: Discussion of the candidates
jokes.punchline: Lenny's nonsensical comparison of Patterson to himself
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist joke that captures working-class political discourse perfectly, highly quotable

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, Patterson's a lot like me. We both have two eyes, a nose, and a mouth. Plus, we both enjoy breathing and not being on fire.

jokes.text: Potato man. Where the hell have you been?
jokes.setup: Homer interrupting U2 concert
jokes.punchline: Band member treating Homer like missing crew member
jokes.explanation: Random absurdist moment that works but feels disconnected from main story

dialogue.speaker: Bono
dialogue.line: Where have you been? We've been looking all over for you!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What? I'm not...

dialogue.speaker: The Edge
dialogue.line: Come on, we're on in five minutes!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But I don't even...

dialogue.speaker: Adam Clayton
dialogue.line: Just get your bass and let's go!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: 'Cause I'd be the most wack tripped-out sanitation commissioner ever! Can you dig it?
jokes.setup: Homer on stage trying to appeal to young voters
jokes.punchline: Middle-aged man using outdated slang embarrassingly
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy showing Homer's attempt to be hip falling flat

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yo, yo, yo! What's up, my young homies? It's me, Homer Simpson, in the hizzouse!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm down with the kids. I'm totally radical. Radical to the max!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Vote for me, and I'll make sure we get more of that phat money for schools and whatnot.

jokes.text: Can't someone else do it? That's perfect
jokes.setup: Homer complaining about needing campaign slogan
jokes.punchline: His lazy complaint becomes his winning slogan
jokes.explanation: Brilliant political satire - laziness as electoral strategy, perfectly captures voter apathy

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I need a campaign slogan, but I'm too lazy to think of one.

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: That's it! 'Too lazy to think' - that's your slogan!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Finally, something that works without effort.

jokes.text: Aren't you tired of having to peel that last snotty Kleenex from the bottom of your wastebasket?
jokes.setup: Homer's campaign speech about annoying chores
jokes.punchline: Specific gross detail everyone relates to
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor that's both gross and relatable, effective campaign rhetoric

jokes.text: Animals are crapping in our houses, and we're picking it up. Did we lose a war? That's not America. That's not even Mexico!
jokes.setup: Homer talking about pet care responsibilities
jokes.punchline: Absurd geopolitical analysis of litter box duty
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalating absurdity that's also sharp political commentary, very quotable

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know, taking care of a pet is a big responsibility. You gotta feed 'em, give 'em water, clean their litter box... It's like being Secretary of State, except the cat doesn't have nuclear weapons. Yet.

jokes.text: Well, then you should have been early
jokes.setup: Patterson explaining he's late because someone tampered with his brakes
jokes.punchline: Homer's illogical response to serious sabotage
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - completely missing the point with confident stupidity

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh! Well, at least they didn't tamper with the donuts!

jokes.text: Well, then you'd better turn up your hearing aid, Pops
jokes.setup: Patterson saying he can't believe what he's hearing
jokes.punchline: Homer making ageist insult despite small age difference
jokes.explanation: Basic political insult that works for character but isn't particularly clever

jokes.text: What button? What the hell are you talking about? What button? Where am I? Who took my false teeth?
jokes.setup: Homer's ageist attack on Patterson
jokes.punchline: Homer acting senile to prove his point
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the age joke, shows Homer's willingness to commit to a bit

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You're discriminating against me because I'm old!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'll prove it — watch this. Who's the president? Uh... is it still that guy? What's his name... the tall one... or was he short?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: And another thing — I can't remember what we were talking about. Is this my house?

jokes.text: If I hadn't already packed my letter opener, I'd give you such a stabbing
jokes.setup: Patterson angry about losing election
jokes.punchline: Threatening violence but in most bureaucratic way possible
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - bureaucrat threatening violence with office supplies

jokes.text: You told people I lured children into my gingerbread house
jokes.setup: Homer claiming they're still friends
jokes.punchline: Reference to Homer's absurd campaign lie
jokes.explanation: Good callback to unseen campaign absurdity, fairy tale reference works well

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: We're still friends, right?

dialogue.speaker: Patterson
dialogue.line: Homer, you told everyone you invented the internet.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, I did invent it. I just didn't tell anyone about it until now.

jokes.text: You told people I lured children into my gingerbread house.
jokes.setup: Homer claiming they're still friends
jokes.punchline: Revealing Homer's bizarre campaign lie
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully absurd political attack comparing opponent to witch from Hansel and Gretel

jokes.text: You are going to crash and burn, my fatheaded friend.
jokes.setup: Patterson predicting Homer's failure
jokes.punchline: Combining prediction with backhanded friendship claim
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Patterson's professional frustration

dialogue.speaker: Ray Patterson
dialogue.line: You're gonna fail, Homer. You always do.

dialogue.speaker: Ray Patterson
dialogue.line: But I like you anyway.

jokes.text: Oh, the garbageman can, Marge. The garbageman can
jokes.setup: Marge questioning if Homer can fulfill his promises
jokes.punchline: Homer's delusional confidence in third person
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's false confidence, sets up inevitable failure

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you always say you're going to do things and then you don't.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's not true! Homer Simpson always follows through on his promises.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: You said you'd fix the gutters three years ago.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Homer will fix those gutters. Homer will do many things.

jokes.text: They let me sign checks with a stamp, Marge. A stamp!
jokes.setup: Marge asking how he spent so much money
jokes.punchline: Homer's childlike amazement at bureaucratic tools
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - amazed by mundane bureaucracy like it's magic

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, how could you spend $5,000 at the office supply store?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But Marge, you should see the things they have! Staplers that staple! Tape dispensers that dispense tape! And the highlighters... they highlight!

jokes.text: That's awful! I almost wish it were drugs.
jokes.setup: Homer reveals illegal dumping scheme
jokes.punchline: Worker preferring drug dealing to environmental crime
jokes.explanation: Good callback with environmental message - dumping worse than drugs

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: We dump all our waste illegally in the river at night.

dialogue.speaker: Worker
dialogue.line: That's terrible! I'd rather deal drugs than be part of something this environmentally destructive.

jokes.text: Other cities don't want it, so they pay me to dump it in this old abandoned mine. That's awful! I almost wish it were drugs
jokes.setup: Homer explaining how he got money to pay workers
jokes.punchline: Marge wishing for drug dealing over environmental destruction
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor escalation - environmental crime worse than drug dealing to Marge

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I sold the blood from my body.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that's disgusting!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I know, but I got three hundred dollars.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, I wish you'd sold drugs instead.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why would you say that?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: At least drug dealing doesn't destroy the environment like all your schemes do.

jokes.text: Marge! Ixnay on the ash holetray
jokes.setup: Marge calling Springfield a trash hole
jokes.punchline: Homer using pig Latin to hide obvious truth
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy - Homer's childish attempt at secrecy about obvious situation

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Springfield is a trash hole!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Igpay atinlay!

jokes.text: You know, I'm not much on speeches, but it's so gratifying to leave you wallowing in the mess you've made. You're screwed. Thank you. Bye
jokes.setup: Patterson reinstated to fix Homer's disaster
jokes.punchline: Polite speech format delivering brutal dismissal
jokes.explanation: Perfect revenge speech - polite format with brutal content, very satisfying

jokes.text: He's right. He ain't much on speeches.
jokes.setup: Patterson's vindictive farewell speech
jokes.punchline: Moe's literal interpretation of Patterson's self-deprecation
jokes.explanation: Classic Moe literal-mindedness missing the bigger picture

dialogue.speaker: Patterson
dialogue.line: I'm a worthless drunk who's wasted everyone's time.

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Well, he said it, not me.

jokes.text: Moving the whole town five miles down the road. It's crazy! Yeah, it's something, all right. So we transplant the town. We're just gonna trash the new Springfield too
jokes.setup: Town's solution to trash problem
jokes.punchline: Recognition they'll just repeat the same mistakes
jokes.explanation: Good environmental/social commentary about not learning from mistakes

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: We solved the trash problem!

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: Yeah, we're really making a difference!

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: Wait a minute... isn't this exactly what we did last time?

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: Oh no, he's right. We're just gonna mess it up again, aren't we?

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: Yep.

jokes.text: How many spoons have you got now, Adam? Nine. If I didn't have my spoons, I'd go insane
jokes.setup: Adam showing off Springfield spoon
jokes.punchline: His obsessive attachment to spoon collection
jokes.explanation: Decent character comedy about obsessive collecting, works as end tag

dialogue.speaker: Adam
dialogue.line: This is my prized Springfield spoon. I've been collecting these for years.

dialogue.speaker: Bono
dialogue.line: That's just a spoon, mate.

dialogue.speaker: Adam
dialogue.line: Just a spoon? JUST A SPOON?! Do you have any idea how rare this is? I have 47 of them at home. I've organized them by year, by material, by the angle of the bowl. I sleep with them in a climate-controlled case. I've named them all. This one's Gerald.

tmdb_overview: Homer feuds with the Springfield garbage men and decides to run for Sanitation Commissioner. After winning the election with a sleazy campaign, Homer can’t keep the extravagant promises he made to the town.
insight: Political satire escalates absurdly as Mayor Quimby's incompetence triggers citywide garbage crisis.
